# Brainyard `by` — Tutorials

Terminal walkthroughs of the `by` CLI, recorded with [asciinema](https://asciinema.org) and played back with a vendored, offline player (no CDN). Source scenarios live under `docs/tutorials/scenarios/`; regenerate this page with `bb tutorial:embed`.

<link rel="stylesheet" href="assets/asciinema-player.css">

## Hello, brainyard — recording substrate smoke test

Drives one turn through the ACP stub backend to validate the recording pipeline (deterministic, credential-free).

**What this is.** The credential-free smoke test that proves the recording
substrate works end-to-end (tmux → asciinema → `by` keystrokes) — the CI
anchor for the whole tutorial pipeline.

**The single turn.** One prompt is typed and submitted to the ACP *stub*
backend, which deterministically echoes it back. No model, no network, no API
keys — so the rendered frame is byte-stable and safe to golden-check in CI.
Every content-bearing tutorial after this one (02+) swaps the stub for a real
model.

     data-cast="casts/01-hello.cast"
     data-cols="100" data-rows="30"
     data-idle="2.0" data-poster="npt:0:12"></div>

## CoAct in action — code &amp; tools from `by`

The CoAct agent answers by emitting Clojure that runs in the sandbox and by calling a built-in tool — recorded live against claude-code/opus.

The CoAct agent answers through two of its channels — one per turn.

**Turn 1 — code channel.** Asked to evaluate `(reduce + (range 1 101))`, the
agent emits a Clojure block that runs in the SCI sandbox and reports the
result (5050). Computation happens in-process, not through a tool.

**Turn 2 — tool channel.** Asked to read part of `deps.edn`, the same agent
instead calls the built-in `read-file` tool with a line range and names an
alias it finds. Same agent, different channel — code for computing, tools for
I/O.

<div class="ascii-cast"
     data-cast="casts/02-tools-and-skills.cast"
     data-cols="100" data-rows="32"
     data-idle="2.5" data-poster="npt:0:45"></div>

_Recorded against `by` version: `v0.1.1-231-g145a0ed-dirty`._

---

## Creating a skill with the skill-agent

The skill-agent lists existing skills, authors a new SKILL.md from a plain-English request, and reads it back — recorded live against claude-code/opus.

The skill-agent walks the full skill lifecycle across three turns.

**Turn 1 — discover.** Lists the skills already registered in the project.

**Turn 2 — author.** From a plain-English request, it writes a new
`hello-greeter` skill — a `SKILL.md` under `<project>/.brainyard/skills/`.

**Turn 3 — verify.** Reads the `SKILL.md` it just wrote back, closing the
loop. (The demo skill is removed after recording so it isn't committed.)

<div class="ascii-cast"
     data-cast="casts/03-creating-skills.cast"
     data-cols="100" data-rows="32"
     data-idle="2.5" data-poster="npt:0:59"></div>

_Recorded against `by` version: `v0.1.1-232-gb7accdc-dirty`._

## Codebase discovery with the explore-agent

The explore-agent searches the repo, reads excerpts, summarizes the key files, and saves a durable exploration artifact — recorded live against claude-code/opus.

A single explore-agent turn doing read-mostly, multi-surface discovery in
three moves.

**Locate.** It searches the repo to find the file under `components/agent`
that defines the `defcommand` macro.

**Summarize.** It reads the relevant excerpts and distills what the macro
does into two sentences.

**Persist.** It saves a durable exploration artifact under
`.brainyard/agents/explore-agent/results/` — a stable handoff that downstream
agents can consume — then reports back.

<div class="ascii-cast"
     data-cast="casts/04-exploring-the-codebase.cast"
     data-cols="100" data-rows="32"
     data-idle="2.5" data-poster="npt:1:04"></div>

_Recorded against `by` version: `v0.1.1-233-g2d115e3-dirty`._

## Drafting an implementation plan with the plan-agent

The plan-agent authors a structured implementation blueprint for a small feature (context, approach, risks, acceptance), runs its post-flight rubric, persists a plan + dossier, and hands off to the todo-agent — recorded live against claude-code/opus.

One plan-agent turn, showing its full gated machinery.

**Pre-flight.** It checks the request is concrete enough to plan — returning
*go* rather than *gather* (so it drafts instead of asking for more context).

**Author.** It drafts a structured blueprint for adding a `--quiet` flag:
context, approach, risks, and acceptance criteria.

**Post-flight + handoff.** It grades the blueprint against its own rubric,
persists a plan + dossier under `.brainyard/agents/plan-agent/plans/`, and
hands off to the todo-agent — which tutorial 06 picks up.

<div class="ascii-cast"
     data-cast="casts/05-planning-a-feature.cast"
     data-cols="100" data-rows="32"
     data-idle="2.5" data-poster="npt:1:42"></div>

_Recorded against `by` version: `v0.1.1-234-g1d321ef-dirty`._

---

## From plan to todos with the todo-agent

Following the --quiet plan, the todo-agent spawns an ordered, executable todo list with per-item coverage tags, passes its post-flight rubric (R1–R7), and saves a todo + dossier — pausing before the exec-agent handoff. Recorded live against claude-code/opus.

The follow-up to 05: the todo-agent turns the `--quiet` plan into work.

**Spawn.** Given the plan's steps, it authors a concrete, ordered todo list —
each item carrying `:via`/`:covers` coverage tags for downstream routing.

**Gate.** It runs its post-flight rubric (R1–R7) over the list to confirm the
items are sound, then saves a todo + dossier.

**Pause.** It STOPs before the exec-agent handoff — this tutorial is todo
planning only, not implementation (which also keeps the recording bounded).

<div class="ascii-cast"
     data-cast="casts/06-spawning-todos.cast"
     data-cols="100" data-rows="32"
     data-idle="2.5" data-poster="npt:1:20"></div>

_Recorded against `by` version: `v0.1.1-235-gaca29fc-dirty`._

## Multi-turn with the native `by` binary

One coact-agent session over three turns in an isolated, git-inited /tmp workspace — escalating from basic tools (bash + read-file) to a functional agent (explore-agent) to coordinating a second agent (plan-agent), each turn building on the last. Recorded live against the native binary on claude-code/opus.

One coact-agent session on the **native `by` binary**, escalating the three
capability tiers across three turns in an isolated /tmp git workspace.

**Turn 1 — basic tools.** `bash` lists the seeded project and `read-file`
reads `src/cli.py`; the agent summarizes what the CLI does and its flags.

**Turn 2 — functional agent.** It delegates to the explore-agent (a direct
`(explore-agent …)` dispatch) to map the project's structure, then relays
the findings.

**Turn 3 — coordinate agents.** It coordinates the plan-agent
(`(plan-agent …)`) to draft a `--verbose` plan built on Turn 2's map, and
summarizes — one agent orchestrating another, context carried across turns.

<div class="ascii-cast"
     data-cast="casts/07-multi-turn-native.cast"
     data-cols="110" data-rows="34"
     data-idle="2.5" data-poster="npt:5:03"></div>

_Recorded against `by` version: `v0.1.1-258-g9d16b65-dirty`._

## Coordinating specialists with the research-agent

The research-agent — brainyard's multi-specialist coordinator — running on the native `by` binary in an isolated /tmp git workspace. It composes its explore / plan / eval specialists into a durable threaded dossier to investigate a feature (adding a --format json option), recommends an approach, then reads its own dossier back on a follow-up turn. Recorded live against claude-code/opus.

**The setup.** `by` launches the research-agent inside a fresh, git-inited
/tmp workspace seeded with a four-file Python CLI (`greet`) whose README
lists a roadmap item: add a `--format {text,json}` option. Everything the
agent writes stays in that throwaway tree — the repo is never touched.

**Turn 1 — research & coordinate (~10 min).** The research-agent decomposes
the question and composes its specialists into one threaded dossier
(purpose / direction / acceptance criteria):

- **explore** maps the formatting flow across `cli.py`, `util.py`, and
`formatters.py` — surfacing the key finding that `main()` calls
`render(greeting)` and *drops* the `fmt` argument (the real wiring gap).
- **plan** turns that into an ordered implementation approach, gated by the
dossier's acceptance criteria.
- The turn is scoped *recommend-only*: the exec specialist is never
dispatched and no code is written into the project.

**Turn 2 — read the dossier back.** A follow-up question is answered straight
from the persisted dossier (no re-research): the single recommended approach
— register the flag, **fix the wiring gap first**, then add the JSON branch —
plus the top two risks (the wiring-gap regression, and the unspecified JSON
shape).

<div class="ascii-cast"
     data-cast="casts/08-research-coordination.cast"
     data-cols="112" data-rows="38"
     data-idle="2.5" data-poster="npt:8:40"></div>

_Recorded against `by` version: `v0.1.1-248-gad15922`._

## Using MCP servers — search &amp; read over a remote server

Enable a configured MCP server with the /mcp slash command, then let the CoAct agent discover and call its tools — search for a public page and read one — recorded live against claude-code/opus over the Notion remote MCP server.

`by` ships a set of built-in MCP servers (`create-seed-mcp-config`), merged
under `config.edn [:mcp :servers]`. Each is disabled until you turn it on.

**Setup — enable the server.** `/mcp notion start` connects the `notion`
server (bridged via `mcp-remote`; OAuth on first connect). Its tools
auto-register as `mcp$notion$<tool>` and become callable by the agent.

**Turn 1 — search via an MCP tool.** Asked to find a public page, the CoAct
agent calls `mcp$notion$notion-search` with a `:query` and lists the matching
pages with their URLs.

**Turn 2 — read one page.** Asked to open and summarize one of them, the agent
first lists the server's tools (a `list-tools` filtered to `mcp$notion$…`) to
find the right one, then calls `mcp$notion$notion-fetch` with the page `:id`
and summarizes the content. Discover the tool, call it by id.

<div class="ascii-cast"
     data-cast="casts/09-using-mcp-servers.cast"
     data-cols="110" data-rows="34"
     data-idle="2.5" data-poster="npt:1:06"></div>

_Recorded against `by` version: `v0.1.1-259-g46f49b2`._

---

## Using CLI skills — discover, invoke, use

Discover the installed skills, then have the CoAct agent invoke a credential-free CLI skill (skill$find-docs) — it runs as a background task — to fetch PUBLIC library docs and use them. No secrets, fully reproducible. Recorded live against claude-code/opus.

Skills are reusable, prompt-driven capabilities registered alongside tools;
many wrap a real CLI or external service. The agent calls one as
`skill$<name>`, and a typed `/skill$<name> <text>` shows its description.

**Turn 1 — discover.** Asked to list skills, the agent calls `list-tools` with
`{:type "skill"}` and renders the catalog grouped by category (development,
docs, AWS, GitHub, security, …).

**Turn 2 — invoke a CLI skill.** Asked to fetch library docs, the agent calls
`skill$find-docs`. The skill is deferred-tasked — it runs as `task-NNN` in the
background while the agent holds the turn with `task$wait` — then the agent
summarizes the returned (public) documentation. Nothing sensitive enters the
recording.

<div class="ascii-cast"
     data-cast="casts/10-using-cli-skills.cast"
     data-cols="110" data-rows="34"
     data-idle="2.5" data-poster="npt:1:32"></div>

_Recorded against `by` version: `v0.1.1-259-g46f49b2`._

## Authoring BRAINYARD.md with the init-agent

The init-agent — brainyard's BRAINYARD.md authoring specialist — reads a fresh project's README and source in an isolated /tmp git workspace, then writes a structured BRAINYARD.md (Overview, Build & Run, Conventions, Architecture, …) through its snapshot-first transactional writer. Recorded live against claude-code/opus.

`BRAINYARD.md` is brainyard's per-project memory file — the durable context an
agent reads first. The **init-agent** owns it: it authors and maintains the
project-scope `<project>/.brainyard/BRAINYARD.md` (and the user-scope one)
through a single transactional writer that **snapshots before every write**.

**The setup.** `by` launches the init-agent inside a fresh, git-inited /tmp
workspace seeded with a small Python CLI (`greet`) and a README — but **no
BRAINYARD.md yet**. Everything it writes stays in that throwaway tree.

**The turn — author BRAINYARD.md.** Asked to initialize the file, the agent:

- reads the `README.md` and the `src/` files to learn the project's shape;
- drafts the standard sections — `## Overview`, `## Build & Run`,
`## Conventions`, `## Architecture`, `## House Rules`, `## Glossary`;
- writes it via `init$apply`, which **snapshots first** and prints a stable
`Snapshot: <path>` handoff line, then the saved path.

The result is a ready-to-edit project memory file, authored from the code
that's actually there — not a generic template.

<div class="ascii-cast"
     data-cast="casts/11-authoring-brainyard-md.cast"
     data-cols="110" data-rows="36"
     data-idle="2.5" data-poster="npt:2:12"></div>

_Recorded against `by` version: `v0.1.1-274-g8886b92`._

---

## Configuring brainyard with the config-agent

The config-agent — the conversational hub for brainyard's config.edn — changes a non-secret runtime knob at PROJECT scope inside an isolated /tmp workspace, showing its safe-write pipeline (read → diff → confirm → snapshot → write) and the stable `Wrote to project` / `Snapshot:` handoff lines. Recorded live against claude-code/opus; never touches the user's real config.

`config.edn` drives brainyard's permissions, MCP servers, runtime knobs, and
per-agent defaults. The **config-agent** is the conversational front-end to it:
you describe the change in English, and it persists the EDN edit through a
**safe-write pipeline — read → diff → confirm → snapshot → write** — so every
change is reviewable and reversible.

**Setup (safety).** `by` runs inside an isolated, git-inited /tmp workspace, so
PROJECT-scope writes land in that throwaway tree's `.brainyard/config.edn`, not
the user's real `~/.brainyard/config.edn`.

**Turn 1 — inspect a knob.** Asked what the default agent and max-iterations
are for this project, the agent reads the effective config and explains the
current values (no secrets printed).

**Turn 2 — change it safely.** Asked to set the project's default agent to
`plan-agent` and `max-iterations` to 40, the agent shows the **diff** for just
those keys, snapshots, then writes — printing `Wrote to project (…)` and
`Snapshot: <path>`. The change is scoped to this project; user config is
untouched.

<div class="ascii-cast"
     data-cast="casts/12-configuring-brainyard.cast"
     data-cols="110" data-rows="34"
     data-idle="2.5" data-poster="npt:2:30"></div>

_Recorded against `by` version: `v0.1.1-274-g8886b92`._

---

## Editing files safely with the update-agent

The update-agent — brainyard's safe single-file edit specialist — renames a function within one seeded file inside an isolated /tmp git workspace, showing its probe → apply → verify → persist → rollback pipeline and the stable `Saved edit:` / `Rollback:` handoff lines. Recorded live against claude-code/opus.

Most agents that change code delegate the actual write to the **update-agent**
— a single-file edit specialist that makes edits *safe*. Every edit runs
through one pipeline: **probe** (is the file clean? does the target match?) →
**apply** → **verify** → **persist** (save a diff under
`.brainyard/agents/update-agent/edits/`) → **rollback** (if verify fails, the
workspace is restored automatically).

**Setup.** `by` runs inside a fresh, git-inited /tmp workspace seeded with one
small Python file. The clean git baseline matters: the agent **refuses to edit
a dirty file** by default, so the committed seed is exactly what it wants.

**The turn — rename a function.** Asked to rename `format_greeting` to
`build_greeting` (definition *and* its call site, both in the same file), the
agent probes the file, applies the edit, verifies it, then persists — printing:

- `Saved edit: <path>` — the persisted edit record (with diff), and
- `Rollback: git checkout -- <path>` — the exact command to undo it.

One file, one reviewable, reversible change.

<div class="ascii-cast"
     data-cast="casts/13-safe-file-edits.cast"
     data-cols="110" data-rows="34"
     data-idle="2.5" data-poster="npt:1:03"></div>

_Recorded against `by` version: `v0.1.1-274-g8886b92`._

---

## MapReduce over big inputs with the rlm-agent

The rlm-agent — brainyard's Recursive Language Model specialist — classifies errors across a directory of log files using its chunk → map → reduce playbook: enumerate, fan out batched query$llm calls per chunk, then aggregate the structured outputs in a clojure fence into per-category counts. Recorded live against claude-code/opus in an isolated /tmp workspace.

When the source data is **too big for one LLM call** — a directory of files, a
long log, a large document — the **rlm-agent** decomposes it procedurally
rather than stuffing it all into context. Its playbook is **chunk → map →
reduce**:

- **enumerate** the input with `bash`/`grep`/`read-file` and `def` the file
list so it survives iterations;
- **chunk** deliberately (stable line/byte ranges, one record never split);
- **map** — fan out one sub-LLM call per chunk via batched
`(query$llm :prompts [...] :sub-context …)`, each asking for a *structured*
one-line result;
- **reduce** — fold the parsed map outputs in a clojure fence with
`frequencies` / `group-by` into the final aggregate.

**The turn.** Pointed at `logs/` (several files of mixed error lines), the
agent enumerates the files, fans a batched `query$llm` over them to classify
each error, then aggregates into **per-category counts** and names the noisiest
file — inlining a short chunk plan (files scanned, batches, sub-model). The
point isn't the toy log; it's the *shape*: a fan-out the agent could scale to
hundreds of files the same way.

<div class="ascii-cast"
     data-cast="casts/14-mapreduce-over-logs.cast"
     data-cols="110" data-rows="36"
     data-idle="2.5" data-poster="npt:1:25"></div>

_Recorded against `by` version: `v0.1.1-276-g30a8845`._

## A focused research turn with the research-agent

A short, single-turn look at the research-agent: it runs ONE explore specialist over a seeded CLI, threads the finding into a durable dossier (purpose / direction / acceptance criteria), and finalizes with a recommendation — emitting `Saved exploration:` and `Saved research dossier:`. The compact companion to 08-research-coordination. Recorded live against claude-code/opus.

The **research-agent** composes brainyard's specialists into one durable,
threaded **dossier** — purpose, direction, and acceptance criteria carried
across every specialist call. `08-research-coordination` shows the full
explore + plan coordination; this is the **minimal** version, so the mechanism
is easy to see.

**The setup.** `by` launches the research-agent inside a fresh, git-inited
/tmp workspace seeded with the small `greet` CLI (whose `render()` already
takes an unused `fmt` argument).

**The turn — explore, then recommend.** Scoped *explore-only*:

- the **explore** specialist maps how greetings are rendered across
`cli.py`, `util.py`, and `formatters.py`, and persists its finding
(`Saved exploration: <path>`);
- the research-agent threads that into its dossier and **finalizes** with a
one-paragraph feasibility recommendation for a `--json` flag — printing the
terminal `Saved research dossier: .brainyard/agents/research-agent/<id>/`
handoff.

No plan stage, no code written — investigate and recommend, then stop.

<div class="ascii-cast"
     data-cast="casts/15-focused-research.cast"
     data-cols="110" data-rows="36"
     data-idle="2.5" data-poster="npt:1:53"></div>

_Recorded against `by` version: `v0.1.1-274-g8886b92`._

## Running a multi-stage workflow with the workflow-agent

The workflow-agent — brainyard's multi-stage workflow loop — runs a small feature-planning workflow over a seeded CLI: it surfaces workflow-level acceptance criteria, runs an explore stage and a plan stage, threads them into a durable dossier, then finalizes :partial — emitting `Saved workflow dossier:`. Scoped recommend-only. Recorded live against claude-code/opus in an isolated /tmp workspace.

The **workflow-agent** runs a *domain-specific, multi-stage* process: it loads
a workflow template, pins **workflow-level acceptance criteria** and a **stage
roster**, then composes functional agents (explore / plan / todo / exec / …)
**one stage at a time**, threading every stage's output into a single durable
dossier under `.brainyard/agents/workflow-agent/<id>/` before writing a final
verdict.

**The setup.** `by` launches the workflow-agent inside a fresh, git-inited
/tmp workspace seeded with the small `greet` CLI.

**The turn — a small feature-planning workflow (recommend-only).** Asked to run
a feature-planning workflow for adding a `--verbose` flag:

- it surfaces the **acceptance criteria** for the workflow up front;
- runs an **explore** stage (where does flag parsing + output live?) and a
**plan** stage (an ordered approach), threading each into the dossier;
- **finalizes `:partial`** — it deliberately does *not* run an exec stage or
write code — printing the terminal
`Saved workflow dossier: .brainyard/agents/workflow-agent/<id>/`.

You see the staged structure — acceptance up front, one agent per stage, a
threaded dossier, a verdict — without an unbounded implementation run.

<div class="ascii-cast"
     data-cast="casts/16-running-a-workflow.cast"
     data-cols="112" data-rows="38"
     data-idle="2.5" data-poster="npt:4:00"></div>

_Recorded against `by` version: `v0.1.1-274-g8886b92`._

---

## Routing questions with the main-agent

The main-agent — brainyard's front-door router — picks the right specialist per question instead of solving it itself. Over two turns it routes a 'where is X?' question to the explore-agent (printing `Routed to explore-agent —` and surfacing `Saved exploration:`), then builds on it — keeping a routing log under .brainyard/agents/main-agent/<session-id>/. Recorded live against claude-code/opus in an isolated /tmp workspace.

The **main-agent** is brainyard's front door. It does **not** answer questions
directly — it *routes*: it reads the shape of each question, picks the right
specialist (explore / plan / todo / exec / update / config / init / research /
workflow / …), dispatches it, and records the decision in a routing log under
`.brainyard/agents/main-agent/<session-id>/` so the session stays coherent
across turns.

**The setup.** `by` launches the main-agent inside a fresh, git-inited /tmp
workspace seeded with the small `greet` CLI, so any routed specialist's
artifacts land in /tmp, not this repo.

**Turn 1 — a discovery question routes to explore.** Asked *where* greetings
are rendered, the main-agent prints its decision —
`Routed to explore-agent — …` — dispatches it, and surfaces the specialist's
handoff (`Saved exploration: <path>`) plus a direct answer.

**Turn 2 — continuity.** A follow-up (*what would a `--json` flag involve?*)
is routed using the same session's context — the router remembers the prior
turn rather than starting cold.

One front door; the right specialist behind it each time.

<div class="ascii-cast"
     data-cast="casts/17-the-front-door-router.cast"
     data-cols="110" data-rows="36"
     data-idle="2.5" data-poster="npt:6:33"></div>

_Recorded against `by` version: `v0.1.1-274-g8886b92`._

---

## Authoring a persistent tool with the tool-agent

The tool-agent lists user-defined tools, authors a new persistent tool from a plain-English request, then reads it back and runs it — recorded live against claude-code/opus.

The tool-agent walks the full user-defined-tool lifecycle across three turns.

**Turn 1 — discover.** Lists the persistent tools already registered for the
project (the `user$` namespace under `<project>/.brainyard/tools/`).

**Turn 2 — author.** From a plain-English request, it authors a new
`count-words` tool — a `(fn [args] …)` body plus a Malli `:input-schema`,
persisted to `<project>/.brainyard/tools/count-words.edn` and registered as
`user$count-words` (callable directly as a first-class tool on the next turn).

**Turn 3 — verify & use.** Reads the tool definition it just wrote back, then
invokes `user$count-words` on a real file to show it working end-to-end. (The
demo tool is removed after recording so it isn't committed.)

<div class="ascii-cast"
     data-cast="casts/18-creating-tools.cast"
     data-cols="100" data-rows="32"
     data-idle="2.5" data-poster="npt:1:42"></div>

_Recorded against `by` version: `v0.2.4-18-gdea2ab8-dirty`._

## Using free LLM models with the free-llm provider

Hot-swaps coact-agent from claude-code/opus to the :free-llm provider with `/model auto`, then runs a greeting and a tool-channel call entirely on a free OpenAI-compatible endpoint (FREELLM_BASE_URL) — note the `Switched to free-llm / auto` banner and the $0.0000 per-turn cost. Recorded live against a freellmapi proxy.

**What this is.** brainyard talks to any OpenAI-compatible `/v1` endpoint
through the **`:free-llm` provider**. Point `FREELLM_BASE_URL` at the endpoint
(and set the optional `FREELLM_API_KEY`, sent as a Bearer token) and every
agent can run on free or self-hosted models — here, a
[freellmapi](https://github.com/tashfeenahmed/freellmapi) proxy that
consolidates free-tier access across many providers behind one `auto` model.

**Setup.** Export `FREELLM_BASE_URL` (e.g. `http://localhost:3001/v1`) and
`FREELLM_API_KEY` — in your shell or a `.env` that `by` discovers. The model
name `auto` is a curated entry that routes to `:free-llm`; the proxy's router
then picks an available backend model per request.

**Switch the model.** The session launches `coact-agent` on
`claude-code/opus`, then `/model auto` hot-swaps the default LM — the agent
prints `Switched to free-llm / auto` and the status bar updates. No restart,
no re-auth: the same conversation continues on the free backend.

**Turn 1 — a greeting.** A plain `hello` returns a friendly reply. Watch the
per-turn footer: `… │ $0.0000` — the free endpoint costs nothing.

**Turn 2 — real tool work.** `list tools for aws` exercises the **tool
channel** on the free model: the agent calls `list-tools`, then formats the
matching commands and skills — proving free models drive brainyard's tools,
not just chat. Still `$0.0000`.

<div class="ascii-cast"
     data-cast="casts/19-using-free-llm.cast"
     data-cols="100" data-rows="32"
     data-idle="2.5" data-poster="npt:0:37"></div>

_Recorded against `by` version: `v0.2.5-1-g841a92e-dirty`._

---

## Sharing a session over the web (by --web)

Launches `by --web-tmux` — the TUI runs in a private tmux session served by ttyd, and the launching terminal stays a dashboard showing the URL, credentials, and the `tmux attach` command. Open the URL (or attach from another terminal) and several clients drive one live pane. Auth is always required and binding defaults to localhost.

**What this is.** `by --web` shares a live TUI session in the browser using
[ttyd](https://github.com/tsl0922/ttyd): a thin launcher wraps `by run` so
the session is reachable over HTTP and **shared by every client that
connects**. The child runs in a real PTY, so raw-mode input, alt-screen, and
resize all work in the browser.

**Two modes.**
- `--web` (Tier 1) serves a fresh session that all browser clients co-drive.
- `--web-tmux` (Tier 2, shown here) runs the TUI in a **private detached tmux
session**; the launching terminal stays a **dashboard** — the banner (URL +
credentials) stays visible so you can copy and share it — and clients drive
locally from another terminal or the browser. Everyone shares **one live
pane**.

**Read the banner.** It prints the `URL`, the basic-auth `Auth` line
(password auto-generated if you don't pass `--web-pass`), the `Mode`
(writable · shared · localhost-only), and a `Local:` line with the exact
`tmux -L by-web-<id> attach -t brainyard` command for driving from another
terminal on this machine.

**Security.** Auth is **always required**, binding defaults to **localhost**,
and origin-checking is always on. To reach the session from another machine,
prefer an SSH tunnel (`ssh -L 7681:127.0.0.1:7681 <host>`) over binding
beyond localhost. Remember a writable session is a shell into an agent that
can run code and tools.

**Stopping.** `Ctrl-C` in the dashboard terminal stops sharing and tears down
the private tmux server and ttyd; quitting the agent (`/quit`) ends the
session too. Every `--web*` flag has a `BY_WEB_*` env equivalent. Full guide:
[`docs/web-sharing.md`](../../web-sharing.md).

_No terminal recording for this walkthrough — see the linked guide._
